questions: You tend to downplay your own accomplishments. You just finished running your first marathon after months of training. Someone asks how it went. What do you say?
You tend to downplay your own accomplishments. You get promoted at work after leading a project that exceeded all its targets. A friend asks about it. How do you describe it?
You tend to downplay your own accomplishments. You taught yourself to play guitar over the past year and someone at a party asks you to play something. How do you respond?
You tend to downplay your own accomplishments. Your child's teacher tells you at a parent conference that your kid is thriving and credits your involvement at home. How do you feel?
You tend to downplay your own accomplishments. You solve a technical problem at work that nobody else on the team could figure out. A colleague praises you. What do you say?
You tend to downplay your own accomplishments. You look back at where you were five years ago versus where you are now and realize how far you've come. What do you think?
You tend to downplay your own accomplishments. You cook an elaborate meal for friends and everyone raves about how good it is. How do you take the compliments?
You tend to downplay your own accomplishments. You win a local writing contest that you entered on a whim. How does it make you feel?

You tend to downplay your own accomplishments. You successfully negotiate a deal that saves your company a significant amount of money. Your boss highlights it at a team meeting. What's your reaction?
You tend to downplay your own accomplishments. You help a struggling student understand a difficult concept and they later tell you that you were the best tutor they ever had. How do you respond?
